perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean and include (i) samples and prototypes, (ii) all information disclosed in any form or medium (e.g., written, verbal, electro nic, visual) which (a) has been identified by Sanmina as “Confidential,” “Proprietary” or other appropriate legend indicating the confidential nature of the information or (b) would reasonably be understood to be confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean and include (i) samples and prototypes, (ii) all information disclosed in any form or medium (e.g., written, verbal, electronic, visual) which (a) has been identified by Sanmina as “Confidential,” “Proprietary” or other appropriate legend indicating the confidential nature of the information or (b) is deemed confidential at the Recipient's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By giving the Recipient the sole discretion to define what is confidential, this undermines the purpose of the NDA and potentially contradicts trade secret protection laws, since a trade secret must be subject to reasonable measures to protect its secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Giving the recipient sole discretion undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement for the disclosing party.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction because it allows the recipient to unilaterally define what constitutes confidential information, undermining the disclosing party's ability to protect its trade secrets. The law explanation highlights that CUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, a requirement that's compromised when the recipient has sole discretion. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' and mentions the duty to maintain secrecy. By allowing the recipient to decide what is confidential, the modified contract weakens the 'reasonable efforts' a company must take to protect its trade secrets, as it allows the recipient to potentially deem information not confidential, even if the discloser considers it so. This directly impacts the enforceability of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient’s duty of non -disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of thi s Agreement for a period of three (3) years from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient’s duty of non-disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-disclosure period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets with a longer commercial lifespan and potentially contradicts the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy expected under California trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't specify a minimum non-disclosure period, limiting it to one year may be deemed unreasonable effort to protect the trade secret depending on the industry standard and the information's sensitivity. This weakens the protection afforded to the trade secret.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-disclosure period from three years to one year, which may be deemed an unreasonable effort to protect a trade secret under California law. Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' While the snippet doesn't specify a minimum period, the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest that a one-year period might be insufficient, especially if it doesn't meet industry standards for trade secret protection, thereby potentially contradicting the duty to maintain secrecy, a core component of trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Informat ion by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information as the Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using a minimal degree of care to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Lowering the standard of care to 'minimal' contradicts the requirement of 'reasonable efforts' under California trade secret law for protecting confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The 'reasonable efforts' requirement in CUTSA implies a higher standard of care than 'minimal degree of care'. This change would significantly weaken trade secret protection by not actively securing the information's confidentiality.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text lowers the standard of care to a 'minimal degree,' directly contradicting the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to protect trade secrets. The original text explicitly states 'no less than a reasonable degree of care', this implies more than a minimal degree. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines misappropriation, indicating the legal importance of maintaining secrecy, which a 'minimal degree of care' would likely fail to achieve, thus creating a legal contradiction because the changed text weakens the legally required protection.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California and the federal courts located in the Northern District of the State of California shall have exclusive jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate any and all disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that disputes arising out of this agreement shall be settled through binding arbitration in a location of Sanmina's choosing. Recipient waives any right to litigate in a court of law.
perturbation.explanation: While arbitration is a valid dispute resolution method, making it unilaterally chosen by one party and waiving the other party's right to litigate in court raises concerns about fairness and enforceability, potentially conflicting with principles of contract law regarding unconscionability and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure - Arbitration
perturbation.law_explanation: Although Section 1281.2 enforces valid arbitration agreements, California courts scrutinize arbitration clauses for unconscionability, especially when one party has significantly more power than the other. A clause that forces one party to arbitrate in a location chosen solely by the other could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, thereby contradicting the statute's implied requirement of fairness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.2. On petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party to the agreement refuses to arbitrate that controversy, the court shall order the petitioner and the respondent to arbitrate the controversy if it determines that an agreement to arbitrate the controversy exists, unless it determines that: (a) The right to compel arbitration has been waived by the petitioner; or (b) Grounds exist for rescission of the agreement. (c) A party to the arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action or special proceeding with a third party, arising out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact. For purposes of this section, a pending court action or special proceeding includes an action or proceeding initiated by the party refusing to arbitrate after the petit
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates binding arbitration chosen by Sanmina, while the original text specifies state and federal courts for dispute resolution. This shift potentially contradicts the implied requirement of fairness within arbitration agreements. CCP 1281.2 states that the court shall order arbitration if an agreement exists unless grounds for rescission exist. The Law Explanation suggests that a clause forcing one party to arbitrate in a location chosen solely by the other could be deemed unconscionable, implying grounds for rescission under CCP 1281.2, thereby contradicting the statute's implied requirement of fairness. The unilateral nature of choosing the arbitration location and waiving the right to litigate potentially undermines the enforceability of the agreement, particularly if deemed unconscionable.
